retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Capsid Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: The phase III clinical trial of lenacapavir involving 72 patients showed no serious adverse effects (grade 3 or above), with only injection site reactions and gastrointestinal symptoms identified. However, given the small numbers involved, post-marketing reports should be reviewed. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Florida HIV Safety for Florida Clinical Laboratory Personnel -- Clinical Significance -- Types of ART Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Each medication targets a step in the HIV lifecycle. A combination of different classes is used to treat patients with HIV, including: Fusion inhibitors – enfuvirtide Entry inhibitors – maraviroc Nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTI) – zidovudine, lamivudine, abacavir, didanosine Nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NtRTI) – tenofovir Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTI) – efavirenz, nevirapine Integrase inhibitors – raltegravir Protease inhibitors (PI) – ritonavir, darunavir, atazanavir [2]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A Recent HIV infection is considered the phase of 6 months or less after infection. [3] An HIV regimen often varies based on potential drug interactions with the patient's current medications and the adverse effects experienced. Pregnant patients should begin treatment immediately to prevent mother-to-child transmission of HIV and protect the woman's health. [4] [5] The following includes all FDA-approved HIV medications: NRTIs: Abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine; tenofovir disoproxil fumarate, zidovudine NNRTIs: Efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine Fusion inhibitors (FIs): Enfuvirtide Protease inhibitors (PIs): Atazanavir, darunavir, fosamprenavir, ritonavir, saquinavir, tipranavir Chemokine receptor 5 (CCR5) antagonist: Maraviroc IIs: Dolutegavir, raltegravir, elvitegravir, bictegravir Postattachment inhibitors: Ibalizumab Pharmacokinetic Enhancers: Cobicistat The following includes all FDA-approved HIV combination medicines:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals constitute a broad pharmacologic group that is organized by therapeutic indication, mechanism of action, and anatomical site of use. Within the hepatitis C subclass, ribavirin and the oligopeptide‑derived telaprevir illustrate the use of peptide scaffolds in antiviral design. The HIV‑combination subclass includes fixed‑dose regimens abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine, both belonging to the larger HIV‑combination antiviral group. Additional antiretrovirals for HIV infection comprise the entry inhibitor enfuvirtide, the fusion‑inhibitor ibalizumab, nucleoside and nucleotide reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) such as abacavir, tenofovir disoproxil, lamivudine, emtricitabine, stavudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine, non‑nucleoside reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) including delavirdine (and delavirdine‑mesylate), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine (the latter contraindicated with pantoprazole), and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antiviral agents that target viral protease enzymes essential for the replication of certain viruses, most notably HIV. Key members of this class include fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which is classified as a protease inhibitor. By inhibiting the protease step in the viral life cycle, these drugs prevent the maturation of infectious viral particles and are integral components of combination antiretroviral therapy used to achieve viral suppression in patients with HIV infection. Understanding that fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir all belong to the protease inhibitor group helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, anticipate class‑related effects, and manage drug‑drug interactions within comprehensive HIV care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors are a class of antiretroviral agents that act by binding to HIV reverse transcriptase and inhibiting viral replication. The drugs efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin, and rilpivirine are all members of the non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same pharmacologic group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanism of action, anticipate class‑related effects, and make informed choices when selecting or switching antiretroviral therapy for patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) are presented as a hierarchical drug family that encompasses agents for hepatitis C virus (HCV), human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and ophthalmic viral infections, organized by chemical class, therapeutic indication and mechanism of action. Within the HCV‑specific DAA subgroup ribavirin and telaprevir are listed; ophthalmic antivirals comprise famciclovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridine and trifluridine, the former two belonging to the “nucleosides and nucleotides excluding reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors” class. HIV‑directed DAAs include the fixed‑dose combinations abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine, as well as individual agents: integrase inhibitors cabotegravir, dolutegravir and raltegravir; non‑nucleoside reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) efavirenz, delavirdine, delavirdine mesylate, rilpivirine, nevirapine, etravirine and nevirapin; nucleoside/nucleotide reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) tenofovir disoproxil,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, comprise a group of medications that target the HIV protease enzyme to prevent viral replication. Key members of this protease inhibitor class include ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each sharing the common mechanism of protease inhibition while differing in pharmacokinetic properties and clinical use. Recognizing these agents as part of the broader protease inhibitor, direct‑acting antiviral category is essential for selecting appropriate antiretroviral regimens and optimizing patient care in HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: ibalizumab, and investigational compounds such as rilpivirine. Therapeutic regimens employ NRTIs (tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine), integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir), NNRTIs (doravirine, delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine), the CCR5 antagonist maraviroc, and the attachment inhibitor fostemsavir. Doravirine is contraindicated when co‑administered with the rifamycin rifapentine. Additional antiviral agents are classified by scaffold: lipopeptidyl‑derived compounds include bulevirtide and caspofungin (the latter also listed among systemic antimycotics with micafungin), while monoclonal antibodies such as abciximab and ibalizumab target specific immune pathways. Bulevirtide, enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab and tecovirimat belong to the ATC “Other antivirals” group; bulevirtide shares the lipopeptidyl scaffold. Remdesivir is used for viral infections, zanamivir for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir are all members of the protease inhibitor class, which constitutes a group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes, thereby blocking the proteolytic processing of viral polyproteins that is essential for viral replication. This mechanism makes them key components of antiretroviral therapy, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where inhibition of the HIV protease enzyme reduces viral load and slows disease progression. Recognizing that fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir share this common pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their collective role in disrupting viral life cycles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 9
retrieved_docs.content: is a structural analogue of deoxyuridine. Idoxuridine and cidofovir are members of the same nucleoside/nucleotide‑exclusion group and possess antiviral prophylactic potential, whereas rimantadine is placed in the “cyclic amines, direct‑acting antivirals” class and alters cellular synthetic activity. HIV infection can be prevented or treated with a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents. Preventive or prophylactic drugs include enfuvirtide, abacavir, delavirdine (and delavirdine‑mesylate), dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, ibalizumab, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine and zidovudine. Treatment‑oriented agents comprise cabotegravir, fostemsavir and maraviroc; cabotegravir, dolutegravir and elvitegravir belong to the systemic integrase‑inhibitor subclass, while tenofovir disoproxil is a nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor that forms part of the backbone of HIV therapy. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1732
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, amprenavir, and tipranavir are all classified as protease inhibitors that belong to the group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug specifically targets viral protease enzymes, a critical step in the replication cycle of viruses such as HIV, thereby providing a mechanism of action that directly interferes with viral maturation. Their shared categorization as direct‑acting antivirals highlights that they act on viral components rather than host cellular processes, which informs clinicians about common therapeutic objectives, potential for cross‑resistance, and similar considerations regarding drug‑drug interactions and adverse effect profiles in antiretroviral treatment regimens.
